CAMS Aerosols in ICON H. Muskatel et al.
CAMS 3D climatology and forecasted aerosols as new options in
ecRAD radiation
Harel Muskatel (1), Daniel Rieger (2), Pavel Khain (1), Martin Köhler (2)
(1) Israel Meteorological Service,
(2) Deutscher Wetterdienst
This document refers to release icon-2024-07.
Abstract
The Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) 3D climatology and forecasted
aerosols are now implemented in ICON model as an input to the ecRAD radiation scheme.
For more than a decade CAMS model was used as a reliable source of air quality forecasting
system around the globe. These kinds of models require huge computational resources. Using
their products as an “offline” model is a reasonable intermediate solution between the costly
fully-coupled aerosol model and the cheaper aerosols climatology. These forecasted aerosols
are interpolated in space and time and treated as a background to the radiation scheme (no
internal advection inside ICON). The second option which is now implemented in ICON, is the
CAMS 3D climatology. It has several advantages over the Tegen 2D climatology as explained
in the introduction. This report contains the description of how to run ICON using each of
the new developments: the 3D CAMS climatology (irad_aero = 7) and the CAMS forecasted
aerosols (irad_aero = 8).
Keywords: Aerosols, Radiation
Correspondence to: Harel Muskatel(muskatelh@ims.gov.il)
1. Introduction
Aerosols in the atmosphere have a significant impact on weather and climate. They directly
interact with both incoming solar radiation and outgoing thermal radiation through absorption
and scattering. Furthermore, they are necessary for cloud formation which effects not only the
radiative fluxes but also precipitation and other atmospheric processes. The direct effect on
radiation can be easily seen in dust outbreaks and volcanic ash eruptions, but even on regular
conditions aerosols concentration can have large day-to-day variability imposing significant errors
when climatological averages are used. For many years, many NWP models including ICON
(Zängl et al., 2015), used the Tegen 2D monthly climatology (Tegen et al., 1997). In this dataset,
the total column AOD of five generalized species of aerosols is available for one wavelength (550
nm). This single number is used to evaluate the optical properties at each vertical level assuming
some exponential vertical density profile. Correction factors are then used to calculate these
properties to other model’s spectral intervals. These assumptions (i.e. constant size distr. and
Reports on ICON - Issue 011 3
CAMS Aerosols in ICON H. Muskatel et al.
chemical composition, no hygroscopical growth) are not needed when using the CAMS 3D aerosols.
The Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) is an ECMWF project, which provides
global air quality analysis and forecast including aerosols and other tracers (Morcrette et al.,
2009). The model is based on IFS and has a coarser spatial resolution but with the same vertical
resolution (137 levels). The output forecast lead time is 5 days with a 3-hours temporal resolution.
3D mixing ratios of 11 aerosols tracers are calculated: 3 size bins of mineral dust, 3 size bins of
sea salt, sulphate, hydrophobic and hydrophilic tracers of both black carbon and organic matter.
The optical properties were pre-calculated by ECMWF for all ecRAD’s thirty spectral intervals.
Another advantage is that the optical properties for the hydrophilic aerosols are relative-humidity
dependent. CAMS climatology (A. Bozzo et al., 2017) is based on the reanalysis (2003-2014) of
CAMS model for the mentioned aerosol species in a 60-levels vertical resolution. In this case,
the vertical profiles are a result of actual dynamics resulting a monthly climatology of aerosols
profiles (A. Bozzo et al., 2017). The dataset contains monthly averages of layer integrated mass
from which the 3D mixing ratios are calculated and used as an input to ecRAD scheme.
We performed several tests using both schemes in regional and global scales and the direct effect
on radiation was evaluated with positive results. In

catalog_entry.summary: This paper discusses the implementation of CAMS 3D climatology and forecasted aerosols as new options in the ecRAD radiation scheme within the ICON model, providing a detailed description of how to run ICON using these developments.
